facade_colour: charcoal-black painted cladding with white gable trim, a turquoise door with pink surround, and bright red metal railings

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: charcoal-black painted cladding with white gable trim, a turquoise door with pink surround, and bright red metal railings
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Recently repainted and customized facade appears maintained, though the decorative treatment is contemporary rather than heritage in character.
photo_observations.window_details: Large replacement picture-style windows flank the entry on the main floor, with two upper windows set into a simplified bay-and-gable composition.
photo_observations.door_details: Bright turquoise front door with small arched lite, framed by pink trim and reached by a short set of concrete steps.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple painted eaves and trim with no pronounced classical cornice.
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Projecting bay window at second floor, painted charcoal-black to match facade; retains original bay-and-gable form with 2 windows in the bay
photo_observations.porch_details: Small raised stoop with contemporary red metal guardrail and concrete steps.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney visible in the cropped facade view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Gable ornament survives in simplified white trim; the strongest visual character now comes from the bold contemporary paint scheme.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Steep front gable with side roof slopes behind
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Extensive repainting in charcoal-black with contemporary accent colours (turquoise door, pink surround, red railings); replacement windows; modern metal guardrails. Bay-and-gable form still intact despite paint scheme.
photo_observations.notes: Photo isolates the dark-painted bay-and-gable house immediately east of the white Nassau Street row.

notes: Photo isolates the dark-painted bay-and-gable house immediately east of the white Nassau Street row.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include bay window, original windows, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Two-storey bay-and-gable row house, c. 1890-1903, extensively repainted in charcoal-black with contemporary accents (turquoise door, pink surround, red metal railings). Double-height projecting bay and cross-gable form remain intact despite replacement windows and modern paint scheme. Party walls on both sides; single dwelling unit. Good condition, well maintained.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
